_meta.name: Chapter 7 Standard Letter Rules
_meta.description: Candidate rule catalog for SECNAV M-5216.5 Chapter 7 standard letter formatting
_meta.note: All entries are placeholders requiring exact manual text before activation

C7-001.source_text: 1. Use the standard letter format or one of its variations to correspond officially within or outside the DoD. 2. The format of the standard letter, with slight variations, sets the pattern for joint letters, multiple-address letters, endorsements, directives, memoranda, etc. Refer to the two-page letter illustrations at figures 7-1 and 7-2. Figure 7-3 contains the formatting techniques to use when preparing a standard letter for use with a window envelope. Figure 7-4 is an example of a joint letter for joint release by two commands. 3. The person whose title appears in the "To:" line is the action addressee. Aside from its one action addressee, the standard letter may have any number of "Via" addressees, "Copy to" addressees, or both. See chapter 8 to prepare a letter with more than one action addressee.
C7-001.plain_language_rule: Standard letter format must be used for all official DoD correspondence. The standard letter format serves as the base pattern for joint letters, multiple-address letters, endorsements, directives, and memoranda. The To line identifies the single action addressee; additional Via or Copy to addressees may be included. Multiple action addressees require Chapter 8 distribution format.
C7-001.renderer_impact: selects standard-letter rendering path and identifies when Chapter 8 multiple-address handling may apply
C7-001.notes: Foundational section defining standard letter applicability and action addressee rules

C7-002.source_text: Margins: 1 inch all around. On letterhead, typing starts more than 1 inch from the top. Footers are 0.5 inch from the bottom of the page. Do not right, center, or full justify text or use proportional spacing.
C7-002.plain_language_rule: Standard letters must have 1-inch margins on all sides (top, bottom, left, right). On letterhead paper, the first line of typing must start more than 1 inch from the top edge. Page numbers and footers on continuation pages must be positioned 0.5 inch from the bottom of the page. Text must not be right-justified, center-justified, or fully justified. Proportional spacing must not be used. Exception: directive header/footer formatting may be retained if included in the source text.
C7-002.renderer_impact: Page geometry configuration in ReportLab canvas; determines BOUNDARY_SPACINGS for top margin (72 pt = 1 inch), bottom margin (36 pt = 0.5 inch for footers), and left/right margins (72 pt = 1 inch each); letterhead rendering must account for typing start position below 1-inch threshold; text must use left justification only with monospaced or fixed-pitch spacing
C7-002.notes: Margin rules are measurable renderer/layout requirements. Top margin: 1 inch (72 pt); Bottom margin for footers/page numbers: 0.5 inch (36 pt); Left/Right margins: 1 inch (72 pt) each. Letterhead typing start must exceed 1 inch from top edge. Text justification must be left-aligned only (no right, center, or full justify). Proportional spacing is forbidden. These are deterministic layout constraints, not advisory guidance.

C7-003.source_text: For text, use 10 to 12 point font size. Times New Roman 12-point is the preferred font style and size for official correspondence, but Courier New may be used for informal correspondence.
C7-003.plain_language_rule: Standard letter body text must use 10 to 12 point font size. Times New Roman 12-point is the preferred font for official correspondence. Courier New may be used for informal correspondence.
C7-003.renderer_impact: Font selection and sizing throughout document body text; ReportLab font configuration must support Times New Roman 12pt as default, with Courier New as alternative for informal correspondence
C7-003.notes: IMPORTANT AMBIGUITY: Section 7-2.1 (C7-002) prohibits 'proportional spacing' but Times New Roman (the preferred font per 7-2.2) IS a proportional font. This creates a direct conflict between sections. The prohibition on proportional spacing in 7-2.1 likely refers to character spacing/kerning settings rather than font type, since the manual explicitly approves Times New Roman which is inherently proportional. Clarification needed: 7-2.1's 'proportional spacing' prohibition should be interpreted as forbidding variable letter-spacing adjustments, not proportional font families. Times New Roman 12pt remains the standard for official correspondence despite this apparent conflict. Font size range of 10-12pt provides flexibility; 12pt is preferred for body text. Header elements may use different sizes per their specific requirements.

C7-004.source_text: Sender's Symbols. Include the following three identification symbols in the upper left corner, blocked one below the other: (1) SSIC; (2) Originator's Code, by itself or in a Serial Number; and (3) Date. The originator's code, by itself or without the serial number, is the originator's office symbol or the hull number of a ship. Each command or activity will determine makeup of the originator's code. Write the date in month-day-year order (e.g., 15 Mar 2024).
C7-004.plain_language_rule: The sender's symbols block must include three elements in the upper left corner, stacked vertically: (1) SSIC code, (2) Originator's Code (either standalone or as part of a Serial Number), and (3) Date in month-day-year format. The originator's code is the office symbol of the drafter or the hull number for ships. Each command determines the specific makeup of their originator's code.
C7-004.renderer_impact: Sender-symbol block positioning at top of page; affects LETTERHEAD to SSIC_DATE boundary spacing; determines vertical stacking order and alignment of SSIC, originator/serial, and date lines; interacts with C7-002 top margin requirements for letterhead paper
C7-004.notes: CRITICAL FOR RENDERER GEOMETRY: This section directly affects header layout and top-of-page spacing. The sender-symbol block uses longest-line anchor alignment (all three lines share the same left starting position). Date format must be D Mon YY (e.g., 15 Mar 2024). Originator code handling is command-specific - renderer should not validate code content, only presence and positioning. When serial number is used (Ser X), it incorporates the originator code. Interaction with C7-002: on letterhead paper, typing starts more than 1 inch from top, so SSIC_DATE boundary must account for letterhead height plus this minimum spacing. AMBIGUITY: Manual states 'blocked one below the other' but does not specify exact spacing between the three lines; current implementation assumes 1 leading unit (14.4 pt) between each line per standard spacing model.

C7-005.source_text: Classified Correspondence. Classified correspondence must be marked in accordance with SECNAV M-5510.30 (Department of the Navy Information Security Program). Classification markings (TOP SECRET, SECRET, CONFIDENTIAL) must appear on the first and last pages of the correspondence. The overall classification marking appears at the top and bottom of each page. Classified correspondence requires additional handling instructions and may require special distribution controls. Refer to SECNAV M-5510.30 and DoDM 5200.01 for specific classification marking requirements, placement, and handling procedures.
C7-005.plain_language_rule: Classified correspondence must follow SECNAV M-5510.30 and DoDM 5200.01 marking requirements. Classification markings (TOP SECRET, SECRET, CONFIDENTIAL) must appear at the top and bottom of each page, with overall classification shown on first and last pages. Additional handling instructions and distribution controls may apply based on classification level.
C7-005.renderer_impact: Classification marking placement at top/bottom of pages; may affect header/footer rendering; requires special handling for classified document workflows
C7-005.notes: CRITICAL DEPENDENCY: This rule delegates authority to external security manuals (SECNAV M-5510.30, DoDM 5200.01). The SECNAV M-5216.5 correspondence manual does not specify detailed classification marking procedures - it references the Information Security Program manual for those requirements. ENFORCEABILITY is marked 'partial' because: (1) classification level determination depends on content analysis per security guidance, not document formatting; (2) specific marking placement details are in SECNAV M-5510.30, not this manual; (3) some classified correspondence handling depends on command-specific security procedures. The renderer should ensure classification markings appear at top and bottom of pages when present, but validation of proper classification level and complete marking compliance requires security program expertise beyond document formatting. This rule should remain candidate until coordination with security program officials confirms implementation approach. DO NOT activate without security program review.

C7-006.source_text: For Official Use Only (FOUO). FOUO information is unclassified information that may be exempt from public release under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA). FOUO markings are required when correspondence contains information that is exempt from mandatory public release but does not require classification. FOUO markings appear on the first page of the correspondence and may be placed in the classification block area or as a separate marking line. The marking 'FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY' or 'FOUO' must be clearly visible. FOUO handling and distribution is governed by organizational policy and may require additional control measures. Refer to SECNAV M-5510.30 and command-specific guidance for FOUO handling procedures. FOUO markings do not override or replace classification markings when both classified and FOUO information are present.
C7-006.plain_language_rule: For Official Use Only (FOUO) markings are required when correspondence contains unclassified information exempt from FOIA public release. The marking 'FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY' or 'FOUO' must appear on the first page, typically in the classification block area or as a separate marking line. FOUO handling follows organizational policy and may require additional control measures per SECNAV M-5510.30 and command guidance. FOUO markings coexist with but do not replace classification markings when both are present.
C7-006.renderer_impact: FOUO marking placement in header/classification block area on first page; may affect spacing if separate marking line is required; does not affect continuation page marking requirements
C7-006.notes: CRITICAL DEPENDENCY: This rule delegates authority to external policy manuals (SECNAV M-5510.30) and organizational/command-specific FOUO handling procedures. The SECNAV M-5216.5 correspondence manual establishes the requirement for FOUO markings but does not specify detailed handling procedures - those are determined by the Information Security Program manual and local command policy. ENFORCEABILITY is marked 'partial' because: (1) FOUO determination depends on content analysis per FOIA exemption criteria, not document formatting; (2) specific marking placement details may vary by command policy; (3) FOUO handling and distribution controls are organization-specific. The renderer should ensure FOUO markings appear on the first page when present, but validation of proper FOUO designation and complete handling compliance requires FOIA/security program expertise beyond document formatting. FOUO is often internal to an organization and handled by organizational policy rather than uniform DoD-wide standards. This rule should remain candidate until coordination with security program officials confirms implementation approach. DO NOT activate without security program review. AMBIGUITY PRESERVED: Manual does not specify exact placement (classification block vs. separate line) - this is command-specific. DO NOT over-design FOUO enforcement in renderer.

C7-007.source_text:
6. "From:" Line

     a. General. Every standard letter must have a "From:" line, except a letter that will be used with a window envelope. To prepare a letter for a window envelope, follow figure 7-3. As a general rule, the "From:" line is composed of the activity head's title and the activity's name. Refer to the three publications listed below for the correct names and mailing addresses for DON and DoD activities.

(1) SNDL

          (2) Active Marine Link, Personal and Family Link, Military Personnel Services Link, Postal Link under reference.

          (3) DoDAAD, DoD Manual 4000.25-6-M.

     b. Converting an SNDL Address to a "From:" Line Address. The "From:" line gives more than a title, but less than a full mailing address. Include enough information to distinguish your activity from other activities that may have the same name, but are in a different city or country. In some cases a one-of-kind title adequately identifies an activity and the location is unnecessary (e.g., "Secretary of the Navy"). The below examples show various way of converting SNDL addresses to "From:" line addresses.

EXAMPLE:

SNDL Entry                From Line

COMMANDING OFFICER        Commanding Officer, Naval Station, Norfolk
NAVAL STATION
NORFOLK VA 23511-6000

COMMANDING OFFICER        Commanding Officer, USS CHUNG-HOON (DDG 93)
USS CHUNG-HOON (DDG 93)
FPO AP 96662-1302

COMMANDING OFFICER        Commanding Officer, Patrol Squadron 45
VP 45
UNIT 60172
FPO AA 34099-5918

     c. Format. Type "From:" at the left margin on the second line below the date line. The text begins two spaces after the colon. If the entry is longer than one line, start the second line under the first word after the heading.

EXAMPLE:

From: Commanding Officer, USS BLUE RIDGE (LCC 19)

From: Commanding Officer, Naval Recruiting District Minneapolis,
         212 3rd Avenue South, Minneapolis, MN 55401-2592

     d. Avoid Multiple Titles. If your commanding officer has more than one title, choose the title that corresponds with the content of the letter.
C7-007.plain_language_rule: Every standard letter must include a From line, except when using a window envelope format. The From line should contain the activity head's title and the activity's name, with sufficient detail to distinguish the activity from others with similar names. The From label is typed at the left margin on the second line below the date line, followed by two spaces, then the sender information. If the entry extends beyond one line, continuation lines align under the first word after the heading. When the commanding officer holds multiple titles, select the title that matches the letter's content.
C7-007.notes: EXCEPTION: Window envelope format letters do not include a From line (see figure 7-3). The From line composition follows SNDL address conversion principles - more than a title but less than a full mailing address. Location information should be included when needed to distinguish activities with similar names. One-of-kind titles like 'Secretary of the Navy' do not require location. Manual specifies 'second line below the date line' for From line placement and 'two spaces after the colon' before sender information. Continuation line alignment is under the first word after the heading (not under the From label).

C7-008.source_text:
7. "To:" Line

     a. General. Address all correspondence to the activity head of an activity. Include the office code or person's title that will act on your letter in parentheses, if known. If the office code is composed of only numbers, add the word "Code" before the numbers. Do not add the word "Code" before an office code that starts with a letter (e.g., "N" or "SUP"). Because frequent turnover in personnel can result in misrouted mail, avoid using the name of an individual in the "To:" line. You may use the complete mailing address and ZIP+4 code if you want the address for a record. If you will be using a window envelope, follow figure 7-3.

     b. Format. Type "To:" at the left margin on the first line under the "From:" line (do not skip a line). Six spaces follow the colon.
C7-008.plain_language_rule: All correspondence must be addressed to the activity head. Include the office code or action officer's title in parentheses when known. For numeric-only office codes, prepend the word 'Code'; do not use 'Code' before alphanumeric codes (e.g., 'N' or 'SUP'). Avoid using individual names in the To line due to personnel turnover. The complete mailing address and ZIP+4 may be included for record purposes. The To label is typed at the left margin on the first line directly below the From line (no blank line between), followed by six spaces before the addressee information. Window envelope format letters follow figure 7-3.
C7-008.renderer_impact: Header section To line rendering; placement on first line below From line (no spacing gap); six-space offset after colon; left-margin alignment; continuation line alignment under first word after heading (inherited from From/Via line formatting rules); window envelope exception handling per figure 7-3
C7-008.notes: CRITICAL PLACEMENT RULE: To line is on the FIRST line under From line - 'do not skip a line' explicitly prohibits any blank line between From and To. This differs from From line placement (which is on second line below date line). Six spaces follow the colon (vs. two spaces for From line). Manual addresses office code formatting: numeric codes get 'Code' prefix, alphanumeric codes do not. Individual names should be avoided in To line due to turnover risk. Mailing address/ZIP+4 optional for record purposes. Window envelope format is an exception case (see figure 7-3). RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER SECTIONS: To line format follows same continuation line alignment rule as From/Via lines (second line under first word after heading) though not explicitly restated here. Chapter 8 (Distribution) governs multiple action addressees - when more than four addressees exist, use Distribution line instead of To line per Chapter 8. To line identifies single action addressee; Distribution line used for multiple action addressees. AMBIGUITY: Manual does not explicitly state continuation line alignment for To line, but states format is similar to From/Via lines - should follow same alignment pattern. DO NOT activate until renderer geometry confirms To line placement at From_baseline + 1 leading unit.

C7-009.source_text:
8. "Via:" Line

a. General. Use a "Via:" line when one or more activities outside of your activity should review a letter before it reaches the action addressee. The format for the "Via:" line is the same as for the "From:" line and "To:" line discussed in paragraphs 5 and 6 above.

b. Format. Type "Via:" at the left margin on the first line below the "To:" line. Five spaces follow the colon. If the entry is longer than one line, start the second line under the first word after the heading.

EXAMPLE:
From: Chief of Naval Operations To: Commander, U.S. Fleet Forces Command Via: Commander, U.S. Pacific Fleet

c. Numbering Via Addressees. Number "Via" addressees if two or more are listed. Follow the chain of command. Routing starts with the addressee listed first.

EXAMPLE:
From: Commander, Destroyer Squadron 23 To: Commander, U.S. Fleet Forces Command Via: (1) Commander, Carrier Strike Group NINE
(2) Commander, U.S. THIRD Fleet (3) Commander, Naval Surface Force, U.S. Pacific Fleet (4) Commander, U.S. Pacific Fleet

d. When one or more activities outside of your activity should review a letter before it reaches the action addressee, and TV-5 Taskers is the mechanism being used for routing the correspondence, do not use a "Via:" line. Prepare the appropriate Action Memorandum as outlined on page 12-4 of this manual.
C7-009.plain_language_rule: The Via line is used when one or more activities outside the originator's activity must review a letter before it reaches the action addressee. The Via label is typed at the left margin on the first line directly below the To line, followed by five spaces before the addressee information. If the entry extends beyond one line, continuation lines align under the first word after the heading. When two or more Via addressees are listed, number them sequentially following the chain of command, with routing starting from the first-listed addressee. When TV-5 Taskers is used for routing correspondence, do not use a Via line; instead prepare an Action Memorandum per page 12-4.
C7-009.renderer_impact: Header section Via line rendering (optional); placement on first line below To line (no spacing gap); five-space offset after colon; left-margin alignment; continuation line alignment under first word after heading; numbered Via addressees when multiple (sequential numbering per chain of command); TV-5 Taskers exception handling (no Via line when using Action Memorandum routing)
C7-009.notes: CRITICAL PLACEMENT RULE: Via line is on the FIRST line under To line - no blank line between To and Via. Five spaces follow the colon (vs. two spaces for From line, six spaces for To line). Manual explicitly states format is the same as From/To lines for continuation line alignment (second line under first word after heading). Multiple Via addressees must be numbered sequentially (1), (2), (3), etc. following chain of command order. Routing hierarchy starts with first-listed Via addressee. TV-5 Taskers is an exception case - when used for routing, do not include Via line; use Action Memorandum per page 12-4 instead. RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER SECTIONS: Via line is optional header element used for routed correspondence requiring intermediate review before reaching action addressee. Chapter 9 (Endorsements) governs Via addressee behavior - Via addressees must forward with endorsement (may be simple 'forwarded' when no opinion needed). Via addressees may take final action, divert routing, or return to originator with explanation. AMBIGUITY: Manual does not explicitly specify exact baseline spacing between To and Via lines beyond 'first line below' - should follow same pattern as From→To (1 leading unit, 14.4 pt). DO NOT activate until renderer geometry confirms Via line placement at To_baseline + 1 leading unit.

C7-010.source_text:
9. Subject Line

     a. General. The subject line consists of a sentence fragment that tells readers what the letter is about. Use normal word order and capitalize every letter after the colon. In correspondence, do not use acronyms in the subject line. If the subject appears elsewhere in the text of the letter, capitalize it using the "Title Case" format. When replying to a letter, repeat the subject of the incoming correspondence in the subject line, unless a change is essential for clarity.

     b. Format. Type "Subj:" at the left margin on the second line under the last line of the previous heading. Three spaces follow the colon. If the entry is longer than one line, align the second line under the first word after the heading.
C7-010.plain_language_rule: The subject line must be a sentence fragment that tells readers what the letter is about. Use normal word order and capitalize every letter after the colon (all caps). Do not use acronyms in the subject line. When the subject appears elsewhere in the letter text, use Title Case capitalization. When replying to a letter, repeat the subject of the incoming correspondence unless a change is essential for clarity. Type 'Subj:' at the left margin on the second line under the last line of the previous heading, followed by three spaces. If the subject entry is longer than one line, align the second line under the first word after the heading.
C7-010.renderer_impact: Header section Subject line rendering; placement on second line below previous heading (no Ref/Encl) or below Ref/Encl block; three-space offset after colon; left-margin alignment; continuation line alignment under first word after heading; all-caps capitalization after colon; acronym prohibition in subject line; Title Case usage when subject is referenced in body text; continuation page header subject repetition with same formatting
C7-010.notes: CRITICAL PLACEMENT RULE: Subject line is on the SECOND line under the last line of the previous heading - this means one blank line between the previous heading (Via, Ref, or Encl) and the Subject line. Three spaces follow the colon (vs. two spaces for From line, six spaces for To line, five spaces for Via line). Manual explicitly requires ALL CAPS capitalization for the subject text after the colon ('capitalize every letter after the colon'). ACRONYM PROHIBITION: Do not use acronyms in the subject line - spell out terms fully. TITLE CASE EXCEPTION: When the subject is referenced elsewhere in the letter body text (not in the subject line itself), use Title Case capitalization format. REPLY BEHAVIOR: When replying to incoming correspondence, repeat the subject of the incoming letter unless a change is essential for clarity. CONTINUATION PAGES: Subject repeats on continuation pages per section 7-2.17 (C7-017) with same wrapping and alignment logic. RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER SECTIONS: Subject line is a required header element. Placement depends on which headers are present: if Ref/Encl are omitted, Subject is directly followed by Body (1 leading unit spacing); if Ref is present, Subject→Ref spacing is 1 leading unit; if Encl is present without Ref, Subject→Encl spacing is 1 leading unit. AMBIGUITY: Manual does not explicitly define what constitutes an 'acronym' for the prohibition - assume standard acronym definition (abbreviated form of multi-word terms like 'NAVY' for 'Department of the Navy'). DO NOT activate until renderer geometry confirms Subject line placement at previous_heading_baseline + 2 leading units (one blank line gap).
